answer:
In the *Manusmṛti* (Chapter 7), *daṇḍa* (the rod)—also equated with royal punishment—is personified as a divine son of *Dharma*, created by the primordial Lord from Brahmā's spiritual luster to protect all beings and enforce cosmic and social order.[^1] It is the king himself in action: the guarantor of the duties (*dharma*) of the four *āśramas* (stages of life) and all subjects, ever vigilant while others sleep.[^2] Through fear of *daṇḍa*, movable and immovable beings adhere to their enjoyments and duties without deviation; without it, the strong would devour the weak, hierarchies would collapse, and chaos would ensue.[^3] Described as dark and red-eyed, the destroyer of sin, *daṇḍa* must be wielded with due consideration of time, place, ability, and sacred texts—otherwise it rejoices subjects when just but destroys everything (including unjust kings and their kin) when misapplied.[^4] Only a pure, wise king with good counsel can wield it effectively; it underpins prosperity in *dharma*, *artha* (wealth), and *kāma* (desire).[^5]

Parallel treatments appear in the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (Chapter 3, on royal duties or *rājadharma*) and *Yājñavalkya Smṛti* (Chapter 1, likewise on *rājadharma*). The *Viṣṇu* echoes *Manusmṛti* 7.25 almost verbatim, portraying the "dark, red-eyed rod of justice (*daṇḍa*)" as fearless in motion under a discerning ruler, ensuring subjects' prosperity.[^6] It fits within broader kingly obligations: just punishment proportional to offense, protection of the varṇas/*āśramas*, and avoidance of excessive severity.[^7]

The *Yājñavalkya* integrates *daṇḍa* into royal policy (*daṇḍanīti*, the "science of the rod/government") as one of four expedients (*upāyas*—conciliation, gifts, dissension, force), to be used judiciously as a last resort alongside the six measures of foreign policy (peace, war, etc.).[^8] *Daṇḍa* (punishment) is *Dharma*'s primordial form for order, yielding heaven/fame/victory when scriptural and just (even against kin who deviate), but ruin when wielded by the greedy; the king personally adjudicates after weighing offense, time, place, etc.[^9]

These texts align *daṇḍa* as the king's coercive instrument for *varṇa*/*āśrama* stability, with *Manusmṛti* 7 most elaborate on its theology and risks.

[^1]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-73f910).

[^2]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-c970b2), [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-48dedc).

[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-55e886), [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-32b6e6), [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-6458d4).

[^4]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-7ac93f), [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-e9e861).

[^5]: *Manusmṛti*, [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-2d8a4d), [Chapter 7](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/07/translation/#p-39e88d).

[^6]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 3](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/#p-636919).

[^7]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, [Chapter 3](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/).

[^8]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-5552ad), [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-20d31d).

[^9]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-ff5556), [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-b01dfa), [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-a0c43a).
